danger_signals.visual_form: Hostile creatures scattered throughout levels (Hickboy, Major Mucus, etc.)
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat requiring evasion or combat

danger_signals.visual_form: Pits, obstacles, underwater mazes, asteroid fields
danger_signals.meaning: Traversal danger requiring platform skill or time awareness

danger_signals.visual_form: Level culmination with distinctive villain character (Psy-Crow, Queen Slug-for-a-Butt, Evil the Cat, Bob the Killer Goldfish, Professor Monkey-for-a-Head)
Peter Puppy as escort in 'For Pete's Sake' level
danger_signals.meaning: Allied character in danger; failure to protect triggers hostile response and health loss

danger_signals.visual_form: Robotic suit worn by Jim over worm body
danger_signals.meaning: Without suit, Jim is ordinary earthworm; suit loss = functional defeat

safety_signals.visual_form: Winning 'Andy Asteroids' racing segment; defeating bosses; completing escort tasks

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Operates with gun and whip mechanics; distinctive appearance as suited worm

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Individual villain designs (Psy-Crow, Queen Slug-for-a-Butt, Evil the Cat, etc.)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Diverse enemy roster with distinct visual silhouettes and characteristics
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Scattered throughout levels; concentrated in boss arenas

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Crow character; appears in racing segments and boss battles
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Competitive rival in 'Andy Asteroids' racing; full boss encounter if player loses race
Can become hostile (visually distressed) if harmed during escort
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: 'For Pete's Sake' level; dependent on player protection

world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Cannot operate as human; powerless state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Robotic suit encompasses worm body; humanoid silhouette
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Gains combat capabilities (gun and whip); can interact with environment as human-level agent

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemy presence escalates across levels; boss encounters increase in difficulty
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies pursue Jim actively; bosses represent organized opposition

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Companion character present in level; visual distinction from enemies
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: NPC requires protection; player must position NPC away from danger; NPC becomes hostile if damaged

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Tube-like environment; perspective shift to behind-view
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Win/loss condition determines next encounter type (progression vs. forced boss fight)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Aquatic level design with navigational complexity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Time pressure + collision limits; resource management challenge

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Princess What's-Her-Name crushed by cow (cow earlier launched into space by Jim)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Subversive ending; victory pyrrhic outcome despite quest success

environmental_storytelling.example: 'New Junk City', 'For Pete's Sake', 'Andy Asteroids', 'Snot a Problem' convey setting tone and thematic content through title
Junkyard, space racing tubes, underwater mazes, bungee-fighting arenas establish varied world geography
Enemies like 'Queen Slug-for-a-Butt', 'Professor Monkey-for-a-Head', 'Major Mucus', 'Bob the Killer Goldfish' communicate surreal/absurdist world aesthetic
Super suit 'falls from sky'; no explanation provided; establishes magical/technological world confluence
Princess rescue quest ending with cow-crushing death communicates satirical tone about game narrative tropes

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Can shoot enemies, whip-swing on hooks, grab and carry NPCs (Peter Puppy), race against rivals
Competitive racing in asteroid-filled tube; AI-driven rival
Full combat antagonist if player loses race
Follows player guidance; requires whip-jump assistance over pits; passive ally
Becomes hostile; attacks Jim; inflicts health damage
Distribute throughout levels; can be avoided or shot; represent attrition threat
Unique attack patterns; level-specific challenge mechanics; gate progression

notes: Earthworm Jim uses visual distinctiveness and character design satire as primary world-state communicators. The game lacks faction-based military/political signaling (no color-coded teams, no territorial markers). Instead, danger/safety manifests through enemy silhouette recognition, level architecture, and NPC vulnerability states. The surreal character names and designs (slug royalty, organ aliens, monkey professors) establish tone rather than political hierarchy. World-state is communicated primarily through: suit-presence visual (Jim's humanoid form), enemy escalation (encounter density), and narrative outcomes (cow death subverts rescue-quest trope). Environmental storytelling relies on absurdist naming and level-type variety rather than environmental clues. No allegiance systems or faction colors documented.
